Web Mining · Sentiment Analysis · Emotion Mining · 2026

Sentiment and Emotion Mining of Anime Reviews from MyAnimeList: A Computational Study of 4,641 User Reviews

Submitted June 2026  ·  Jikan v4 API  ·  VADER & DistilRoBERTa  ·  TF-IDF + Logistic Regression  ·  Google Colab
Md. Shimul Ahmed Bappi Undergraduate Aspiring Researcher Department of Statistics and Data Science
Jahangirnagar University, Savar, Dhaka 1342, Bangladesh
shimulbappi.sds49@gmail.com GitHub LinkedIn ResearchGate
4,641Reviews Analysed
231Anime Titles
86.4%Positive Sentiment
77.4%Classifier Accuracy
7Emotion Categories
Abstract

User-generated reviews on anime-tracking platforms encode rich, fan-specific affective signals that remain comparatively under-studied relative to mainstream film and product review corpora. This study presents a sentiment and emotion mining analysis of 4,641 user reviews spanning 231 distinct anime titles, collected from MyAnimeList via the unofficial Jikan v4 API. Sentiment polarity was quantified using the VADER lexicon-based model, and fine-grained emotion was detected using the transformer-based j-hartmann/emotion-english-distilroberta-base model. Results show that 86.4% of reviews carried a strongly positive VADER compound score (> 0.5), consistent with the well-documented positive skew of enthusiast fan communities. Joy (mean intensity 0.394) and sadness (0.227) emerged as the dominant emotions, reflecting anime's characteristic blend of uplifting and melancholic narrative content. A Pearson correlation of r = 0.450 was observed between VADER compound scores and official MAL user ratings (1–10 scale). A TF-IDF + Logistic Regression baseline classifier achieved 77.4% accuracy on three-class sentiment classification (Negative, Neutral, Positive), with strongest performance on the Positive class (F1 = 0.90). Genre-based analysis further revealed systematic variation in audience reception across genre categories. These findings demonstrate the value of combining lexicon-based and transformer-based NLP techniques for characterising fan sentiment in niche entertainment domains.

Index Terms: anime MyAnimeList sentiment analysis emotion detection VADER DistilRoBERTa TF-IDF logistic regression web mining NLP
Table of Contents
  1. Introduction
  2. Background and Related Work
  3. Methodology
  4. Results
  5. Discussion
  6. Conclusion
  7. References
I Introduction

Anime — Japanese animated media spanning television series, films, and original video animation — has grown into a globally distributed entertainment category with an active, highly engaged online fandom. MyAnimeList (MAL) is the largest community-driven anime and manga database and review platform, hosting millions of user-submitted ratings and long-form written reviews [1]. Unlike star ratings alone, free-text reviews capture nuanced opinions, emotional reactions, and qualitative judgments about narrative, animation, characters, and pacing that numeric scores cannot convey. Mining this textual corpus offers an opportunity to quantify audience sentiment and emotional response at a scale unattainable through manual reading.

Sentiment analysis and emotion mining have matured into established sub-disciplines of natural language processing, with widespread application to product reviews [9], social media [3], and film criticism. However, anime fandom discourse exhibits distinctive linguistic characteristics — genre-specific terminology, strong franchise loyalty, and a markedly positive baseline sentiment characteristic of enthusiast communities — that merit dedicated empirical study rather than direct extrapolation from general-domain sentiment research.

This study combines a lexicon-based sentiment model (VADER) with a transformer-based emotion classifier (DistilRoBERTa) to jointly characterise polarity and discrete emotion in anime reviews, and additionally trains a supervised TF-IDF + Logistic Regression baseline to assess the feasibility of automated three-class sentiment classification on this domain. The study's specific objectives are: (i) to collect a representative corpus of anime reviews via the Jikan v4 API; (ii) to quantify sentiment polarity using VADER; (iii) to detect fine-grained emotion using a fine-tuned DistilRoBERTa model; (iv) to measure the correlation between computed sentiment and official MAL user scores; (v) to train and evaluate a baseline machine-learning sentiment classifier; and (vi) to examine variation in audience reception across anime genres.

II Background and Related Work

A. Sentiment Analysis of User Reviews

Sentiment analysis refers to the computational identification and categorisation of subjective opinion within text [9]. Early work by Hu and Liu [10] established the foundational approach of mining product reviews for opinion polarity at the feature level, a paradigm later extended to film, restaurant, and e-commerce review domains. The VADER model, proposed by Hutto and Gilbert [3], was purpose-built for short, informal, social-media-style text and incorporates grammatical heuristics — capitalisation, punctuation emphasis, degree modifiers, and emoticons — to produce a normalised compound polarity score between −1 and +1, without requiring domain-specific training data.

B. Transformer-Based Emotion Classification

The introduction of BERT by Devlin et al. [4] established the transformer encoder as the dominant architecture for contextual language understanding. RoBERTa [6] subsequently refined BERT's pre-training procedure, and distilled variants such as DistilBERT [5] reduced parameter count while retaining the majority of upstream task performance, enabling practical deployment for large-scale text mining. Hartmann et al. [7] fine-tuned a DistilRoBERTa-base model for multi-class emotion classification across the Ekman [8] taxonomy — anger, disgust, fear, joy, neutral, sadness, and surprise — the seven discrete categories adopted in this study.

C. TF-IDF and Classical Text Classification

Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) weighting [11] remains a robust and computationally efficient method for representing documents as sparse numerical vectors for downstream classification. Combined with a linear classifier such as logistic regression [12], TF-IDF pipelines provide an interpretable and lightweight baseline against which more complex neural architectures can be benchmarked — a role this study assigns to the supervised classification component.

D. Anime and Fandom Review Mining

Compared to mainstream film and product review mining, computational study of anime-specific review corpora remains comparatively limited, despite the genre's large, vocal, and internationally distributed online fan base. MyAnimeList's open review archive, accessible programmatically via the community-maintained Jikan API [2], provides a tractable corpus for addressing this gap and for examining whether sentiment dynamics observed in general review domains generalise to niche entertainment fandoms.

III Methodology

A. Data Collection

Review data were harvested from MyAnimeList using the Jikan v4 unofficial REST API [2], accessed via the Python requests library. A custom MALDataCollector class retrieved (i) the top-ranked anime titles from MAL's official rankings and (ii) a randomly sampled set of additional titles, in order to balance popular, high-visibility series against a broader cross-section of the catalogue. For each candidate title, up to 25 user reviews were retrieved along with the associated MAL score, helpful-vote count, submission date, genre tags, release year, and episode count. After deduplication and removal of incomplete records, the final corpus comprised 4,641 reviews across 231 distinct anime titles.

B. Text Pre-processing

Raw review text was cleaned by lowercasing, removal of HTML artefacts and URLs, and normalisation of whitespace and punctuation prior to downstream modelling, producing a parallel cleaned_review field used for both sentiment scoring and TF-IDF vectorisation.

C. Sentiment Analysis (VADER)

The VADER SentimentIntensityAnalyzer [3] was applied to each cleaned review to compute a compound polarity score in the range [−1, +1]. Reviews were further categorised into three sentiment classes (Negative, Neutral, Positive) for use as ground-truth labels in the supervised classification stage.

D. Emotion Detection (DistilRoBERTa)

Fine-grained emotion classification used the pre-trained j-hartmann/emotion-english-distilroberta-base model [7] via the Hugging Face Transformers library [13], with inputs truncated to a maximum of 512 tokens. The model returned a probability distribution over seven Ekman emotion categories [8] — joy, sadness, anger, fear, surprise, disgust, and neutral — for each review, from which average intensities were aggregated across the corpus.

E. Sentiment–Rating Correlation

The Pearson correlation coefficient was computed between each review's VADER compound score and its corresponding official MAL numerical score (1–10 scale) to assess the degree of agreement between lexicon-derived sentiment and explicit user ratings.

F. Baseline Machine-Learning Classification

A supervised baseline classifier was trained using TF-IDF vectorisation [11] of the cleaned review text as input features and the VADER-derived three-class sentiment label as the target. A Logistic Regression classifier [12], implemented in scikit-learn [14], was trained on a stratified train/test split and evaluated via accuracy, per-class precision, recall, and F1-score, with results summarised in a confusion matrix.

G. Genre-Based Analysis

Reviews were associated with one or more genre tags supplied by the MAL metadata for each title. MAL score distributions were compared across the most frequently occurring genres to examine whether audience reception varies systematically by genre category, and score distributions were additionally examined by release year.

IV Results

A. Dataset Overview

The final analysed dataset consisted of 4,641 anime reviews spanning 231 distinct anime titles, collected from MyAnimeList using the Jikan v4 API. Reviews spanned a wide range of release years and genre categories. The average official MAL user score across all reviewed titles was 7.94 (SD = 2.41), reflecting a corpus weighted toward moderately-to-highly rated series, consistent with MAL's ranking-driven sampling strategy.

4,641Reviews231 anime titles
7.94Mean MAL ScoreSD = 2.41 (1–10 scale)
0.749Mean VADER Scorecompound, −1 to +1

B. Sentiment Analysis

VADER lexicon-based sentiment analysis showed a strong positive skew typical of enthusiast anime fandom discourse. 86.4% of reviews had a compound sentiment score greater than 0.5, while only 11.4% were negative (compound < −0.05). The Pearson correlation between VADER compound scores and official MAL ratings (1–10) was r = 0.450, indicating a moderate positive relationship: reviews that scored a title more highly numerically also tended to be expressed in more positively-toned language, though the moderate (rather than strong) correlation suggests textual tone and numeric rating capture partially distinct aspects of reviewer opinion.

Fig. 1. Distribution of VADER compound sentiment scores across 4,641 anime reviews. The corpus is heavily right-skewed, with the large majority of reviews clustering near the maximum positive score (+1.0) and a smaller secondary cluster at the most negative extreme (−1.0).
Fig. 2. Distribution of official MAL numerical scores (1–10) underlying the reviewed titles. Scores are concentrated at the upper end of the scale, with the modal score of 10 accounting for the largest single bin.
Fig. 3. Correlation between official MAL score and VADER compound sentiment score (r = 0.450). Each point represents one review; the regression line indicates a moderate positive association between numeric rating and textual sentiment polarity.

C. Emotion Mining

Multi-label emotion classification using the j-hartmann/emotion-english-distilroberta-base model [7] revealed the following average emotion intensities across the corpus: joy (0.3935), sadness (0.2271), surprise (0.1691), neutral (0.0809), fear (0.0567), anger (0.0471), and disgust (0.0256). Joy was the clearly dominant emotional signal, consistent with the positively-skewed VADER distribution; however, sadness registered as the second-most prevalent emotion at a substantially higher average intensity than anger or disgust, suggesting that anime reviews frequently engage with emotionally weighty or bittersweet narrative content even within an overall positive evaluative frame.

Fig. 4. Average emotion intensities across 4,641 anime reviews, as classified by DistilRoBERTa. Joy (0.394) and sadness (0.227) are the two most prominent emotional signals, followed by surprise (0.169).

D. Baseline Machine Learning Classification

A TF-IDF + Logistic Regression baseline model achieved 77.40% accuracy on the held-out test set for three-class sentiment classification (Negative, Neutral, Positive). The model performed particularly well on the Positive class (precision = 0.91, recall = 0.88, F1 = 0.90), reflecting both the larger sample size of positive reviews and the relatively distinctive lexical signature of strongly favourable text. Performance on the Negative class was moderate (F1 = 0.62), while the Neutral class proved most difficult to classify (F1 = 0.48), consistent with the inherent ambiguity of moderately-toned review language.

Table I  ·  Classification Report — TF-IDF + Logistic Regression
ClassPrecisionRecallF1-scoreSupport

Fig. 5. Confusion matrix of the TF-IDF + Logistic Regression baseline classifier on the held-out test set (n = 929). The model correctly classified 550 of 625 Positive reviews, but exhibited greater confusion between the Negative and Neutral classes.

E. Genre-Based Analysis

Audience reception, as measured by official MAL score, varied across genre categories. Genres such as Award Winning, Sports, Slice of Life, and Horror exhibited comparatively narrow, higher-median score distributions, while broader, high-volume genres such as Action, Drama, and Fantasy showed wider spread reflecting the larger and more heterogeneous set of titles tagged with these categories. Drama (1,963 reviews) and Action (1,847 reviews) were the two most frequently occurring genre tags in the corpus, followed by Comedy (1,092) and Adventure (1,002).

Fig. 6. MAL score distribution by top genres (box plots, score range 1–10). Niche genres with smaller review counts (e.g., Sports, Slice of Life, Award Winning) show tighter, higher-centred distributions than high-volume genres such as Action and Drama.
Fig. 7. MAL score distribution by anime release year. Reviewed titles span from 1980 to 2026, with the large majority of release years exhibiting median scores between 7 and 9, indicating a corpus skewed toward well-regarded productions across both classic and recent eras.
V Discussion

The results converge on a broadly positive picture of anime fandom sentiment on MyAnimeList, with 86.4% of reviews registering strongly positive VADER compound scores and joy as the dominant detected emotion. This positive skew is consistent with patterns reported in other enthusiast review communities, where users self-select to review titles they have actively chosen to watch and are disproportionately likely to hold favourable prior dispositions toward the medium.

The moderate sentiment–rating correlation (r = 0.450) is a noteworthy finding: while textual sentiment and numeric score move broadly together, the relationship is far from perfect. This gap likely reflects reviews that are textually critical in tone (e.g., detailed discussion of narrative flaws) while still being accompanied by a relatively high numeric score, or conversely, reviews using emotionally charged but non-negative language (e.g., discussing sad plot developments in admiring terms) that VADER may not penalise. This pattern is corroborated by the emotion mining results: sadness was the second most prevalent emotion overall (0.227), well above anger (0.047) or disgust (0.026), suggesting that much of the affective intensity in anime reviews stems from engagement with melancholic or emotionally weighty story content rather than negative evaluation of production quality.

The baseline TF-IDF + Logistic Regression classifier's strong performance on the Positive class (F1 = 0.90) against comparatively weaker Negative (F1 = 0.62) and Neutral (F1 = 0.48) performance is consistent with the class imbalance inherent to the corpus (625 of 929 test reviews were Positive) and with the generally subtler lexical signal separating Neutral from adjacent classes. This pattern mirrors well-documented challenges in three-class sentiment classification more broadly, where the "neutral" category is typically the hardest to discriminate.

The genre-based analysis suggests that audience reception is not uniform across content categories: genres with smaller, more curated review samples (e.g., Sports, Slice of Life) display tighter and higher score distributions than broad, high-volume genres (Action, Drama), which may partly reflect a survivorship effect — niche genres reviewed on MAL are more likely to represent titles that have already attracted a dedicated and favourably-disposed fan following.

Limitations: (i) The corpus was sampled via a combination of top-ranked and randomly selected titles, which may not be fully representative of MAL's complete review archive. (ii) VADER, as a general-purpose lexicon, may not fully capture anime-specific terminology, sarcasm, or fandom-internal references. (iii) The DistilRoBERTa emotion model truncates inputs beyond 512 tokens, potentially omitting affective content in longer reviews. (iv) Emotion scores were computed for a representative subsample of the corpus rather than every review. (v) The TF-IDF + Logistic Regression baseline used VADER-derived labels as ground truth rather than independently human-annotated sentiment, which may propagate any systematic biases of the lexicon-based model into the supervised classifier's evaluation.

VI Conclusion

This study demonstrates that combining lexicon-based sentiment analysis, transformer-based emotion detection, and classical supervised classification provides a comprehensive characterisation of audience sentiment in anime review text. Applying this integrated pipeline to 4,641 reviews across 231 anime titles collected from MyAnimeList via the Jikan v4 API, this research establishes that: (i) anime review sentiment is strongly positively skewed (86.4% positive); (ii) joy and sadness are the two dominant detected emotions, reflecting the genre's characteristic emotional range; (iii) textual sentiment correlates moderately (r = 0.450) with official numeric ratings, indicating partial but incomplete overlap between the two signals; (iv) a TF-IDF + Logistic Regression baseline can classify three-class sentiment with 77.4% accuracy, performing best on the Positive class; and (v) audience reception varies meaningfully across genre categories.

The methodological framework — combining VADER, DistilRoBERTa-based emotion classification, TF-IDF feature extraction, and a Logistic Regression baseline — offers a replicable template for computational fan-sentiment analysis applicable to other niche entertainment domains and review platforms. Future work should extend this framework with human-annotated ground-truth sentiment labels, incorporate more sophisticated neural classifiers (e.g., fine-tuned transformer classifiers) for the supervised sentiment task, examine temporal sentiment trends across release seasons, and explore review-helpfulness-weighted sentiment scoring analogous to like-weighted approaches used in social media sentiment research.

Gratitude is extended to the open-source communities behind the Jikan API, NLTK, Hugging Face Transformers, VADER, and scikit-learn, and to MyAnimeList for maintaining the underlying review archive.
